abstract: The vast chemical diversity of natural antioxidants complicates the isolation, detection, and quantification of individual compounds within complex food and biological matrices. Furthermore, evaluating total antioxidant capacity is often more biologically relevant than measuring single species, as the health-promoting effects of these compounds typically arise from their synergistic interactions. Despite this, the lack of standardized quantification protocols means that no single assay is currently universally accepted for food labeling. Existing methodologies are generally categorized into electron transfer (ET) and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanisms; however, results between these assays are rarely comparable due to variations in redox potentials, pH sensitivity, and solvent dependencies. This project aims to bridge these gaps by systematically characterizing the properties and mutual interactions of antioxidants. By establishing a more rational framework for classifying assays and addressing their inherent constraints, this work strives to enhance the comparability and clarity of antioxidant measurements. To achieve this, the task group synthesizes diverse expert experiences across multiple analytical platforms to provide a unified perspective on the field.
